title: 13 Hidar — St. Basalide & The Assembly
narrative:
narrative": "narrative\": \"Hidar

St. Basalide (Monthly Commemoration)

St. John Chrysostom (Departure)

St. Damianus (Damianos) the Patriarch

The thirteenth day of Hidar carries the monthly commemoration of St. Basalide — the soldier who converted in the act of observation, whose apostolic vesting came through watching the courage of those in his custody — and places him beside the Departure of St. John Chrysostom, the Golden-Mouthed Archbishop who died in exile after being driven from the throne of Constantinople for the specific apostolic sin of preaching too clearly about the obligations of wealth. Basalide and Chrysostom together present the community with two modes of the apostolic vesting that Hidar has been distributing: the vesting of the simple man whose holiness was kindled by what he witnessed, and the vesting of the orator whose gifts made him a target of every power that preferred its comfort to its conscience. Both were vested for cost; both paid it; both are commemorated in the month of the Apostolic Vesting as testaments to what the commission actually entails.

The Departure of John Chrysostom is among the most painful events in the history of the early Church's institutional life — the condemnation and exile of the most gifted preacher the Greek-speaking Church had produced, engineered by a coalition of imperial and ecclesiastical resentment, accomplished through a corrupt synod, and ending in the bishop's death in the remote Pontic highlands of Comana. He died reportedly on his way to a further, more extreme place of exile, having been forced to walk through conditions designed to exhaust him. His final words — 'Glory to God for all things' — are among the most frequently cited in the Eastern tradition, a sentence that gathers everything the apostolic vesting means into a single act of eucharistic surrender. The one who has been vested for cost offers, in the moment of cost's completion, the thanks that the eucharist expresses — not because the circumstances are pleasant but because the One who gave the commission is good.

St. Damianus, the sixth-century Patriarch of Alexandria, was one of the great figures of the Miaphysite tradition — a theological partisan, a community organizer, and a pastoral administrator who guided the Coptic Church through one of its most complex and contested periods. His patriarchate extended for more than thirty years, during which he navigated the competing pressures of imperial theological policy, internal theological controversy, and the practical demands of leading a large and geographically dispersed Christian community. In the day after Michael's Great Feast, Damianus is the post-feast pastor — the one who carries the grace of the great celebration forward into the ordinary work of governance, who translates the angelic commission into the administrative decisions that sustain the community's mission across its ordinary days.

Basalide, John Chrysostom, and Damianus together on the thirteenth day of Hidar present the apostolic vesting in its most honest and most demanding forms: the quiet conversion through witness, the golden speech that becomes a target, and the long administrative faithfulness that sustains the institution through decades of competing pressures. The Great Feast of Michael has passed; the community now carries its grace into the ordinary work of the remaining month. 'Glory to God for all things' — Chrysostom's dying word — is Hidar's mid-month counsel: whatever the apostolic commission costs, whatever form the vesting takes, whatever the outcome of the faithful work, the eucharistic response is the only response adequate to the One who gave the commission.
